coredata.dc:title: Fabrication, characterization and performance evaluation of an amplified electrochemical sensor based on MOFs nanocomposite for leukemia drug Imatinib determination
coredata.dc:creator: Habibi, Biuck
Pashazadeh, Sara
coredata.dc:description: In this research, the synthesis, fabrication steps and characterization of a new modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with CuFe2O4 magnetic nanoparticles combined with zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF-8) embedded graphene quantum dots (GQDs), (CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs), for electrodetermination of the imatinib (IMB), anti-cancer drug, are reported. The synthesized nanocomposite was characterized using X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM). The GCE modified with CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs nanocomposite shows high electrocatalytic activity toward the oxidation of IMB compared with the bare GCE, GQDs/GCE, ZIF-8@GQDs/GCE, CuFe2O4/GCE and CuFe2O4@GQDs/GCE. The resulted electrocatalytic activity of the CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs/GCE can be attributed to the synergistic effect of the outstanding magnetic, electrical properties of the CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs and high conductivity of GQDs, as well as the high electron transport rate of the nanocomposite. Under optimized conditions, the developed sensor, CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs/GCE, has static properties such as wide linear range responses (0.02–11.85 and 11.85–94.20 μM), low detection limit (1.2 nM), high sensitivity (5.655 μA μM−1), long-term signal stability and reproducibility (RSD = 2.23%). It should be noted that the CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs/GCE sensor could be used for determination of IMB in biological and pharmaceutical samples, indicating its feasibility for real analysis.

head.ce:title: Fabrication, characterization and performance evaluation of an amplified electrochemical sensor based on MOFs nanocomposite for leukemia drug Imatinib determination

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electroanalytical Chemistry Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University, Tabriz 53714-161, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Electroanalytical Chemistry Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Electroanalytical Chemistry Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University, Tabriz 53714-161, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this research, the synthesis, fabrication steps and characterization of a new modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with CuFe2O4 magnetic nanoparticles combined with zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF-8) embedded graphene quantum dots (GQDs), (CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs), for electrodetermination of the imatinib (IMB), anti-cancer drug, are reported. The synthesized nanocomposite was characterized using X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM). The GCE modified with CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs nanocomposite shows high electrocatalytic activity toward the oxidation of IMB compared with the bare GCE, GQDs/GCE, ZIF-8@GQDs/GCE, CuFe2O4/GCE and CuFe2O4@GQDs/GCE. The resulted electrocatalytic activity of the CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs/GCE can be attributed to the synergistic effect of the outstanding magnetic, electrical properties of the CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs and high conductivity of GQDs, as well as the high electron transport rate of the nanocomposite. Under optimized conditions, the developed sensor, CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs/GCE, has static properties such as wide linear range responses (0.02–11.85 and 11.85–94.20 μM), low detection limit (1.2 nM), high sensitivity (5.655 μA μM−1), long-term signal stability and reproducibility (RSD = 2.23%). It should be noted that the CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs/GCE sensor could be used for determination of IMB in biological and pharmaceutical samples, indicating its feasibility for real analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: One of the most common diseases that distress many people around the world is cancer. Cancer is a relatively fatal disease that can progress silently in people's bodies and affect the normal life of a person by involving different organs [1]. In fact, cancer cells multiply uncontrollably and spread throughout the body. In other words, a cancerous tumor is a disease in which some cells in the body grow uncontrollably and spread to other parts of the body [2]. Cytostatic drugs are one of the most common drugs used in cancer treatment, which act to inhibit the growth of cell lines or kill cells directly [3–5]. The main problem in using of cytostatic drugs is the high doses, which are excreted in the patient's urine after 24 h and cause some side effects such as environmental toxicology (often in urban wastewater with concentration up to 100 μg/l) [6]. Imatinib (IMB), with commercial name of Glivec is a first-generation drug that commonly used as an anti-cancer drug [7,8]. In 2001, the United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approved the use of IMB for treatment of various cancers in the United States [9]. Since then, its consumption has increased in the United States and whole world, and now widely prescribes by the world's medical centers. Therefore, the determination of IMB values is necessary both in the field of clinical and environmental toxicology due to the dependence of plasma concentration with the cytogenetic and molecular response of IMB [10]. Until now, several analytical approaches include spectroscopic [11,12], chromatographic [13–15] and electrophoretic methods [16,17] have been developed for the analysis and determination of IMB in real samples. As all know, the techniques mentioned above are methods that require a lot of time and high cost instruments, therefore, electrochemical methods can be used as an alternative method for determination of IMB. Due to the presence of electroactive sites on the surface of IMB molecules, the electrochemical method can be proposed for the detection of this drug via suitable modified electrodes [9,18–22]. Nowadays, among the modifiers for the preparation of modified electrodes, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have received more attention due to their special structural characteristics (such as great surface area, easy preparation and exposed metal sites) [23–28]. MOFs are made by coordination of metal ions (or clusters) and organic linkers that form a repeating cage-like structure that causes the MOFs to have a large internal surface area. Due to their excellent properties, MOFs have been used in various fields. When MOFs are used in their pure form, good results are not obtained in electrochemistry since these materials have low intrinsic conductivity [29]. By combining MOFs with materials that have high conductivity (such as carbon-based materials), the electronic conductivity of these nanoporous crystalline materials can be improved. As a result, the obtained nanocomposites have high conductivity and electrocatalytic activity due to the synergistic effect between the constructed materials [30–33]. Among the available carbon materials, graphene has received much consideration during the last decade. In various research fields, graphene-based nanomaterials (such as graphene oxide, graphene nanoribbons, and graphene quantum dots) have received much attention [34–37]. On the other hand, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) nanocomposite with magnetic nanoparticles have been used in drug delivery, energy storage and removal of pollutants from wastewater [38,39]. Ferrites are a group of magnetic materials important for technological applications and fundamental studies. Magnetic nanoparticles with the general formula MFe2O4-type spinel nano ferrites (M = is a divalent metal such as Fe, Co, Cu, Ni, etc.), a group of inorganic biomaterials, that are the most well-known compounds in analytical biochemistry, medicine, biotechnology and separation methods [40–43]. To the best of our knowledge, CuFe2O4 magnetic nanoparticles/zeolitic imidazolate framework-8@graphene quantum dots nanocomposite (CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs) has not been used for the determination of IMB so far, and it is the first time that their efficiency is investigated for IMB assay. In this research, the CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) as an innovative electrochemical sensor, CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs/GCE was investigated to determine the IMB. The physicochemical characterization of CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs nanocomposite was done using spectroscopic and electrochemical techniques (such as SEM, EDX, XRD and EIS). The developed electrochemical sensor shows excellent performance for the trace detection of IMB with high sensitivity, long-term stability, desirable selectivity, wide concentration range, low detection limit and good reproducibility in the optimized experimental condition. Eventually, the presently developed sensor was able to monitor the IMB concentration in the actual sample (pharmaceutical and biological samples).

A brief description of chemicals and reagents is presented in the Supporting Information. The electrochemical measurements were performed using a conventional three-electrode glass cell system electrochemical workstation with the modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) (diameter: 3 mm), platinum wire (Pt) and Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) utilized as working, auxiliary, and reference electrode, respectively in a computer-controlled electrochemical workstation [Autolab, Eco Chemie, Utrecht, The Netherlands and data processing software (GPES, software version 4.7)] at room temperature (20–25 °C). Morphological and surface compositions of the CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs nanocomposite were investigated by scanning electron microscopy equipped with X-ray energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDX) (Phenom prox). X-ray powder diffraction analysis (XRD; D8 Advance Brucker AXS, Karlsruhe, Germany, CuKα radiation, λ = 1.5406 Å) was utilized to characterize the crystal structure and composition of the prepared nanocomposite. High performance liquid chromatograph Agilent 1260 Infinity II LC system was utilized for the analysis human blood serum samples to validation of the provided electrochemical method.

In this research, microwave and thermal methods were used for the synthesis of GQDs. Briefly, 5 g of citric acid was dissolved in 5 mL of distilled water, then the obtained solution was placed in a microwave oven at 180 degrees Celsius (inside a Teflon kettle, 150 W and for 8 min). Finally, the obtained product (viscous brown liquid) was filtered using a microporous membrane after cooling at room temperature [44,45].

ZIF-8 was synthesized according to the reported method with some modifications [46]. First, a solution containing 35.3 mmol of 2-methylimidazole in 50 mL of methanol was prepared (solution A). Also, a solution containing 4.4 mmol of zinc nitrate hexahydrate in 50 mL of methanol was prepared (solution B). In the next step, solution A was added drop by drop to solution B and the resulting mixture was stirred at room temperature (3 h) and then rested for 24 h in a fixed place. The precipitate was centrifuged and washed with methanol for three times and dried for 8 h at 60 °C [46].

According to the reported method by the researchers [34], 4.4 mmol of zinc nitrate hexahydrate +4 mL of alcoholic GQDs suspension (1 mg/mL) [2, 4, 8, 10, 15, 20%Wt of GQDs based on the weight of the final product (pure ZIF-8 in methanol), 4%Wt was selected as the optimal amount] was added to methanol (50 mL), and sonicated (15 min) (solution A). Then a solution containing 35.3 mmol of 2-methyl imidazole in 50 mL of methanol was prepared (solution B). In the next step, solution A was added drop by drop to solution B. The rest of the steps are done like the previous step. Finally, the obtained ZIF-8/GQDs nanocomposite was dried in an oven (60 °C and 6 h). The synthesis of ZIF-8 is similar to that of GQDs/ZIF-8 mentioned above, except that the alcoholic solution of GQDs is not added to solution B during the preparation process [34,46].

To prepare the CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs nanocomposite, the certain amount of as prepared of ZIF-8@GQDs (0.45 g) and polyvinyl pyrrolidone (0.1 g) was first weighed and dispersed in 100 mL deionized water using ultrasonic (30 min). Then, copper sulfate (10 mmol) and ferric chloride (20 mmol) in 200 mL solution was added at room temperature to the suspension of ZIF-8@GQDs. The amount of ZIF-8@GQDs should be adjusted to obtain a ZIF-8@GQDs to CuFe2O4 weight ratio equal to 3:1. Subsequently, under vigorous stirring a 1 M NaOH solution was gradually added to the reaction vessel until the pH of the mixture reached to 11. Then, the contents of the reaction vessel were heated to 70 °C under stirring for 1 h. The resulted magnetic nanocomposite; CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs, was separated by a magnet and then washed with ethanol and deionized water and finally dried. Likewise, the CuFe2O4 nanoparticles were prepared by the above procedure without adding ZIF-8@GQDs. The synthesis procedure of CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs nanocomposite was shown in Scheme 1 [34,46,47].

To prepare the glassy carbon electrode modified with CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs, in the first step, the unmodified glassy carbon electrode was polished with alumina slurry using a special polishing pad followed by ultrasonication in ethanol and then distilled water for 10 min. For electrochemical activation of the bare GCE, the GCE was placed in a 0.1 M H2SO4 solution and 20 cycles at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1 were cycled in the potential range of −600 to 2000 mV. In the next step, 10 μL of the nanocomposite suspension (CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs) (1 mg/mL in 1:1 ethanol: distilled water solution) is dripped at the surface of the activated GCE and dried in air to obtain a modified electrode (CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs/GCE). A schematic representation of the modified electrode preparation is shown in Scheme 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and determination of IMB in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Performance evaluation of the CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs/GCE in electrodetermination of IMB in human blood serum samples and the preparation procedure of real samples is given in detail in the Supporting Information. Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) technique and standard addition method were used to determine the IMB in real samples [three times assay of each sample (n = 3)]. Also, for check the quantitate results of the real sample analysis in human blood serum by the proposed method with HPLC method as the reference method [48], the chromatographic analyses were performed using LC system with a Zorbax Extend-C18 column (5 μm, 80 A, 4.6 × 250 mm) and a guard column (4.6 × 12 mm) of the same material coupled to a UV/Vis detector. Optimized mobile phase consisted of methanol and aqueous triethylamine (pH 10.5; 1%, v/v) (60:40, v/v). The pH of the aqueous phase was adjusted with 1 M hydrochloric acid. The mobile phase was delivered in isocratic elution mode at a flow rate of 1 mL/min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical characterization of the CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: Since the morphological and compositional properties of materials play an important role in determining their physical and chemical properties, SEM analysis was performed to obtain information about the topographic details. Typical morphology of synthesized materials: (A) ZIF-8, (B) ZIF-8@GQDs, (C) CuFe2O4 and (D) CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs was photographed by SEM were shown in Fig. 1 . As can be seen, the appearance morphology of ZIF-8 is cubic (image A). When GQDs is added to ZIF-8, the morphology changes and becomes a rhombic dodecahedron (image B) [34–37,49,50]. Considering that the preparation of composite using polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) is a common method and according to the available report [51], this change in morphology may be related to PVP effects. In the synthesis steps, GQDs are first added to the zinc ions solution, and in the next step, ligand (imidazolate) is added. Hence, GQDs are spontaneously accumulated and distributed during growth of the MOFs. Encapsulation happens with physical and chemical absorption; physical adsorption on ZIF-8 and chemical adsorption of GQDs interaction with Zn ions [52]. As a result, they cause the formation of ZIF-8@GQDs crystals with nano size and dodecahedron morphology. According to the results reported in the articles [52–55], it can be concluded that the morphology of ZIF-8 is influenced by the type of solvent, type of zinc cation salt, the molar ratio of the materials and the type of synthesis method. Fig. 1 image C illustrated the SEM image for CuFe2O4 magnetic nanoparticles with slight aggregation (mean average particle size = 45 nm). Also, image D illustrated in Fig. 1, represents the obtained SEM image for the CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs nanocomposite. The surface morphology of ZIF-8@GQDs has undergone changes, where the surface looks deformed and unclear, especially in the resulting nanocomposite. Furthermore, the EDX spectrum of CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs (Fig. 1 image E) clearly reveals the presence of Cu, Zn, Fe, C and O elements in the sample which represents the successful synthesis of CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs.

XRD analysis is mostly used to analyze crystal structures and chemical composition of the synthesized materials. Fig. 2 shows the XRD patterns of the GQDs (a), CuFe2O4 (b), ZIF-8 (c) and CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs (d). The regular porous crystalline structure of ZIF-8 and magnetic nanocomposite particles case clear reflections in XRD patterns between 2θ values of 0–60 degrees [56]. The ZIF-8 sample shows perfect reflections, which can be assigned to (011), (002), (112), (022), (013), (222), (114), (233), 25.7° (224), (134), (044), (244), and (235) planes that emphasize the successful formation of ZIF-8 according to previous research [34,55–57]. Also, no obvious gross peaks are found in the XRD pattern, indicating that the resulting ZIF-8 is relatively pure. For the ZIF-8@GQDs, the obtained pattern matched well with the literature [55]. The relative value and prominent peak positions are the same as the values reported in the literature [34,46,47,55–57]. The XRD pattern of the ZIF-8@GQDs nanocomposite very clearly shows the presence of GQDs in the diffraction peaks of nanocomposite at 2θ = 20–30°, which indicates the successful synthesis of hybrid nanocomposite [34,57]. The XRD pattern of pure CuFe2O4 sample shows three distinct diffraction peaks at 2θ of 17.81°, 30.19°, 35.35° and 43.0° (related to plates (111), (220), (311) and (400) respectively) which corresponds to CuFe2O4 tetragonal nanoparticles, with the JCPDS standard (JCPDS No. 34–0425) [58]. For the CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs nanocomposite, the complete characteristic packs of pure constitutes; CuFe2O4, GQDs and ZIF-8, are clear appeared in the final nanocomposite pattern.

In order to electrochemical characterization of the CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs nanocomposite and determine the real surface area of modified electrodes, the cyclic voltammetric method was utilized to evaluate the execution of unmodified and modified GCE electrodes. The catalytic surface areas for the modified GCE at various potential scan rates (10–100 mV s−1) were recorded in KCl (0.1 M)/ Fe(CN)6 3−/Fe(CN)6 4− (5.0 mM) as redox probe solution (Fig. 3A). The electrode real surface areas were determined based on plots [peak current versus (scan rate)0.5] and Randles–Sevick formula is given as [59]:
(1)

In the above equation, Ip, n, A, C, D, and V are the anodic peak current, the number of electrons in the reaction (n = 1), electrochemical active surface area, electroactive species concentration (5.0 mM), the diffusion coefficient of [Fe (CN)6]3−/[Fe(CN)6]4− (7.6 × 10−6 cm2s−1) [60], and the scan rate, respectively. Based on the calculation, the areas found are 0.059, 0.108, 0.128, 0.184, and 0.206 cm2 for the bare GCE, ZIF-8/GCE, ZIF-8@GQDs/GCE, CuFe2O4/GCE, CuFe2O4/ZIF-8/GCE and CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs/GCE, respectively.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) technique is used to investigate the changes that occur at the interface between the electrode surface and the solution. In other words, in this method, the potential applied to the system is disturbed by the modulation of a small sine wave, and the response is measured as a sinusoidal current as a function of the modulation frequency. The kinetic behavior of the probe on the modified electrode interface is defined to prove the connection of the immobilized layers with the charge transfer resistance (Rct) (the change of the diameter of the semicircle recorded in the Nyquist diagrams) [59]. The Nyquist plots of various step by step modified electrodes have been performed by employing [Fe(CN)6]3−/[Fe(CN)6]4− as redox probe in KCl solution, which variability in Rct values related to the surface modification of the electrode compared to the unmodified electrode. As shown in Fig. 3B, the charge transfer resistance (Rct) are estimated to be 145.83, 101.07, 86.18, 65.19 and 49.12 Ω.cm2 for unmodified GCE, ZIF-8/GCE, ZIF-8@GQDs/GCE, CuFe2O4/ZIF-8/GCE and CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs/GCE respectively. According to the results, it can be seen that the CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs/GCE (with equivalent circuit as shown in inset of Fig. 3B) shows a smaller distinct semi-circular arc with a low Rct value compared to other step-by-step modified electrodes indicates that the CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs nanocomposite can improve the charge-transfer kinetics, which implicates the excellent electrocatalytic activity of the designed modified electrode [61].

In order to evaluate the application and reliability of the modified electrode, it is necessary to study the electrocatalytic properties of the prepared electrode. The electrocatalytic activity of the present modified electrodes toward the drug analyt (IMB) was investigated using cyclic voltammetric technique. For this purpose the CV gram of the IMB (30 μM) was recorded at the bare GCE (a), GQDs/GCE (b), ZIF-8/GCE (c), ZIF-8@GQDs/GCE (d), CuFe2O4/GCE (e), CuFe2O4/ZIF-8/GCE (f) and CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs/GCE (g) in the potential window of 0.45 to 1.0 V vs Ag/AgCl in phosphate buffer solution (PBS) (pH = 7.0) with a scan rate of 100 mV s−1 (Fig. 4 ). As shown in Fig. 4, each CV curve has oxidation peak with peak current as 28.53 μA (unmodified GCE), 37.53 μA (GQDs/GCE), 70.03 μA (ZIF-8/GCE), 146.43 μA (ZIF-8@GQDs/GCE), 165.43 μA (CuFe2O4/GCE), 187.63 μA (CuFe2O4/GCE) and 286.43 μA (CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs/GCE). Curve g, the CV gram of the CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs/GCE, gives a well-defined peak for drug oxidation with highest current compared to all the modified GCEs. This shows that the oxidation process of IMB on the GCE surface is significantly increased by the presence of the CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs nanocomposite due to the high conductivity and high active surface of GQDs and synergistic contributions of CuFe2O4, GQDs and ZIF-8 in CuFe2O4/ZIF-8/GQDs nanocomposite, which leads to excellent electrotransfer ability and electrocatalytic activity [62]. On the other hand, an excellent increase in the current response implies that the CuFe2O4/ZIF-8/GQDs/GCE accelerates the electron transfer rate of the electrochemical reaction, increases the peak current and makes possible the quantitative determination of trace concentration of anti-cancer drugs, IMB.

Finally, as can be seen in Fig. 4, no cathodic peak is observed for the IMB redox system during the reverse scanning, so based on scientific findings, it can be concluded that the electrochemical reaction of IMB molecules on all the electrodes is totally irreversible [9,62,63].

Some important and key factors in each electrocatalysis process affect the obtained signal intensity such as the amount of modifier, pH, scan rate, and accumulation time. Loading of the certain amount of CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs modifier can increase the peak current of the resulted electrode and enhance the electrochemical sensitivity. Therefore, different amounts of modifier were examined in the electrode modification process (0.1 to 3 mg/mL). The relationship between Ip and the amount of CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs was evaluated using different amounts of modifier in the electrode modification process (DPV technique, concentration of IMB = 10 μM) (Fig. 5 ). The obtained results show (inset of Fig. 5) that the modified electrode in 1 mg/mL of modifier provides a highest current signal compared to other modifier values due to the increased electrode surface area for IMB oxidation. While a further increase of the modifier causes a decrease the adhesion of the coated layer to the electrode surface and consequently a decrease in peak current because the thickness of the coated layer on the electrode surface increases [20,59–61,64]. This behavior may be attributed to the fact that amounts higher than 1 mg/mL cause roughness on the surface, blocks the electrode surface and prevents IMB from penetrating the electrode surface, resulting in flattened noses and reduced sensitivity. Hence, the amount of 1 mg/mL was chosen as the optimal value for subsequent experimental tests in IMB electrocatalysis and determination.

To find out more information about the possible kinetic mechanism of the IMB electrooxidation process at the CuFe2O4/ZIF-8/GQDs/GCE, the effect of the potential scan rate on the peak current response was evaluated by cyclic voltammetric technique in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.0) in the presence of 30 μM IMB and the obtained results shown in Fig. 7 A. The analysis of anodic peak current in terms of scan rate (Fig. 7B) shows a well linear relationship between peak current (IP) and scan rate (υ), and its linear corresponding equation is I(μA) = 0.8436 + 46.839v (υ in mVs−1), (R2 = 0.9907), which indicates that the electrode reaction is a surface-controlled process [60], suggesting an adsorption-controlled procedure of IMB electrooxidation. This process can be justified by the fact that IMB molecules, as a pyridine-based compound, can chelate with the open metal sites of the ZIF-8 [62,63]. After adsorbing of the IMB molecules on the electrode surface, the electrochemical oxidation of the drug occurred. Furthermore, the relationship between the Epa and scan rate are also investigated. The results showed that with increasing of scanning rate, the peak potential shifted to the more positive potentials. A plot of Epa versus ln υ was plotted (Fig. 7C) and the linear relationship obtained shows that the electrode reaction is irreversible in nature [59,60]. For an irreversible and adsorption-controlled electrode process, Epa can be defined according to the existing equation (according to Laviron's theory [69]).

Where n, F, R, T, α, E0, k0 parameters have their conventional and usual meaning [n = number of transferred electrons; F = Faraday constant (96,485.3C eq−1); R = universal gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1); T = thermodynamic temperature (298 K); α = transfer coefficient; E0 = formal redox potential; and k0 = standard heterogeneous rate constant]. According to the mentioned equation, the value of (1-α) n was calculated using the slope of the Ep-ln υ graph equal to 0.0296 (according to Fig. 7C). Based on the fact that for irreversible electrochemical reactions, the value of α is often considered to be 0.5, the value of n for IMB was obtained as 1.8–2, which is in good agreement with the proposed mechanism of oxidation of IMB at pH 7 on the surface of designed modified electrode (Scheme 2) [62].

Optimization of accumulation time is very important in studies where the proposed mechanism is adsorption process [60]. Because this factor can affect the amount of IMB molecules accumulated on the electrode surface. Fig. 8 depicts the diagram of the anodic peak current (DPV method) in terms of the accumulation time for the 10 μM IMB solution under optimal conditions in the range of 10–150 s by the DPV technique, where a linear relationship between anodic peak current and time was observed. The Ip value increased rapidly with increasing pre-concentration time and reached an uppermost value at 60 s, while peak current remained constant with a further increase in accumulation time. Therefore, in the following experiments, the optimal accumulation time of 60 s was selected.

The ability of the sensor against interfering species (due to the reducing or rising effects on the sensor response) plays an important role in determining the anticancer drug, IMB, on the surface of the proposed sensor. In the study of interference, species are usually selected that have oxidation or reduction peak near the analyte. The ant interfering of the sensor is especially important in biological fluids. Therefore, anti-interference studies of the proposed sensor, CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs/GCE, were carried out for the selective detection of 10 μM IMB simultaneously with a concentration of >10 times of interfering species [including cations, anions, vitamins, amino acids and drug (Metoclopramide)] by DPV method (DPV grams were shown in Fig. S1A). According to the results, the interfering species cannot affect the detection result of IMB, indicating that the proposed modified electrode has high selectivity (Fig. S1B). Therefore, it is possible to measure this IMB in the presence of these ions or compounds without any interference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility and stability of the CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: A rapid evaluation of the reproducibility study was carried out through different electrodes under optimal experimental conditions. Therefore, for the reproducibility study, eight identical electrodes modified by CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs nanocomposite were prepared and used to parallel determination of IMB (10 μM) by DPV technique (Fig. S1C). The relative standard deviation (RSD) for the response between the electrodes was calculated to be 2.23%, which indicates excellent reproducibility of the modified electrode preparation. Also, the storage life or stability of the proposed sensor was evaluated during a long term operation period. Analysis of 10 μM IMB solution in 0.1 M PBS (at a pH level of 7.0) by DPV technique (Fig. S1D) over 28 days (the electrochemical signal was obtained every day) showed a stable current response of the sensor while maintaining 98.12% of the initial response, indicating that the proposed sensor has excellent stability because the response of the designed sensor was almost constant. According to the obtained results, it can be said that the high reproducibility and stability of the modified electrode indicate that the above features clarify the prospective investigations of the sensor in real sample experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical applications of the CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs/GCE in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the applicability and feasibility of the constructed sensor, it is necessary to analyze a real sample in the determination of the analyte with an admissible recovery. To evaluate the analytical and actual application of the newly designed sensor, IMB was determined in real samples (human blood serum and IMB Tablet samples) under optimal conditions by DPV technique and the obtained results were listed in Table 2 . The sample preparation of the real samples was described in the supporting information. The obtained recovery range values of the human blood serum are ±98.80–100.50% with RSDs of 2.88%. Therefore, it can be concluded that the proposed nanocomposite modified electrode, CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs/GCE, has a significant fit that can be used to detect IMB in real samples with a satisfactory recovery. Also, to validate the provided electrochemical method and its results in the human blood serum samples, the quantitate determination of IMB via CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs/GCE sensor was compared to those obtained by the HPLC technique in Table 2. The values obtained from electrochemical procedure agree well with those obtained by HPLC technique in Table 2. The achievements were statistically assessed employing the F-tests. F-Values of 3.38 was achieved for IMB, demonstrating no significant difference between the measured values with two methods at the 98% confidence level. On the other hand, this reveals no significant difference and/or systematic errors regarding the variation between the DPV method and the HPLC method at the 98% confidence level. The favorable precision and accuracy voltammetric determination of IMB using the CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs/GCE sensor shows the applicable capacity of the designed modified electrode for analysis of real samples.

In this work, firstly we synthesized the CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs nanocomposite. In the next step, the GCE was modified with the new synthetic CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs nanocomposite, and then it was used for the selective and sensitive detection of IMB. The synthesized nanocomposite were characterized by different techniques including SEM, XRD, EDX, CV, and EIS. Electrochemical studies showed that the CuFe2O4/ZIF-8@GQDs/GCE has excellent electrocatalytic activity for the oxidation of IMB compared to bare GCE, GQDs/GCE, ZIF-8@GQDs/GCE, CuFe2O4/GCE and CuFe2O4@GQDs/GCE due to the more active sites, large surface area, high conductivity, and fast electron transfer of nanocomposite. On the other hand, the electron transfer ability was significantly increased by adding GQDs to the CuFe2O4 magnetic nanoparticles and ZIF-8, which could introduce a new electrocatalytic material. Further, the newly designed electrode has excellent analytical performance for IMB detection, with low oxidation potential (0.812 V), better LOD (1.2 nM) and LOQ (4.1 nM), wider linear range (0.02–11.85 and 11.85–94.20 μM), excellent sensitivity (5.655 μAμM−1), long-term stability, satisfactory selectivity and reproducibility. Most importantly, due to the excellent conductivity and good stability of the designed sensor, the prepared nanocomposite can certainly be a promising candidate for various perspectives to develop advanced sensing systems for a wide range of electrochemical applications and bioassays, compounds and pharmaceutical analytes.
